coredata.dc:title: Confined intercalating sulfonated graphene quantum dots into GO laminates for fast alkali recovery
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Jingjing
Hong, Ziqiang
Zhang, Suixin
Li, Yong
Jia, Rui
Tan, Ruonan
Shao, Hongwei
Zhang, Shichao
Li, Xinxin
Ran, Jin
Cui, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Graphene oxide (GO) membranes with uniform sub-nanometer interlayer channels have shown great promise in the ion sieving field. However, the lack of ion-transporting sites on the GO nanosheets usually leads to inferior ion permeation and selectivity. Although the intercalation of molecules with ionic groups into GO laminates is easily achieved to incorporate ion exchange sites, this approach inevitably enlarges the interlayer spacing, reducing the ionic sieving capacity. Thus, current methods are limited in breaking the permeation and selectivity “trade-off”. Herein, we report a confined intercalation strategy to inject ample sulfonic acid groups into GO laminates using sulfonated graphene quantum dots (SGQD) as intercalators. SGQD with abundant ion exchange sites assembles on the sp2 domains of GO via π-π interactions and confers the GO membranes with continuous ion conduction pathways. Meanwhile, benefiting from the ultra-small size SGQD as well as its sheet morphology, it can well accommodate in the GO laminates. This strategy fulfills the incorporation of ionic sites without expanding channel width and destructing the two-dimensional (2D) regularity for GO laminates, resulting in simultaneous improvements in ion permeability and selectivity. Correspondingly, the resulting membranes show synergistically improving OH- dialysis coefficients of 3.5 × 10−3-8.2 × 10−3 m h−1 and OH-/WO4 2- separation factors of 56–116 compared with the GO membrane (0.9 × 10−3 m h−1 and 8.4) for separating simulated NaOH/Na2WO4 alkaline effluents. We believe the confined intercalating regulation can act as a versatile and fine way to modify 2D membranes for intensifying ion separation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfonated graphene quantum dots
Confined intercalation
Two-dimensional membranes
Graphene oxide
Alkali recovery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQD, SGQD, GO and PSS
Preparation of GO, GO/SGQD and GO/PSS membranes
Alkali recovery performance evaluation using the diffusion dialysis (DD) process
The structure and physio-chemical properties of GO/SGQD membranes
Investigations of fast separation mechanisms for GO/SGQD membrane

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagrammatic sketch of the fast OH− transport and efficient OH−/WO4 2− selectivity in GO/SGQD membranes by the confined intercalation strategy.

(a) Preparation process of SGQD. (b) FTIR spectra of GQD and SGQD. (c) XPS spectra of GQD and SGQD (inset table: the element amounts of C, O, N and S in GQD and SGQD respectively). (d) and (e) High-resolution XPS spectra of S 2p and C 1s of SGQD, respectively.

(a) and (b) TEM images of GQD and SGQD (insets are lateral size distribution of GQD and SGQD), respectively. (c) and (d) High-resolution TEM images of GQD and SGQD, respectively. (e) AFM image of SGQD on the mica slice surface. (f) Height profile of SGQD along the white line in Fig. 3e.

(a) Illustration of π-π interactions between SGQD and GO nanosheets. (b) Fluorescence phenomenon of the SGQD solution. (c) Fluorescence quenching phenomenon of the GO + SGQD mixed solution. (d) High-resolution TEM image of the pre-assembled SGQD on the GO nanosheet surface. The sample for TEM observations was washed with water three times. (e) and (f) Elemental mappings of C and S elements for Fig. 4d. (g) pH-dependent zeta potentials of the GO, GQD and SGQD dispersions.

(a) and (b) Surface SEM images of GO and GO/SGQD-2 membranes, respectively. (c) Cross-sectional SEM image of the GO membrane. (d) Cross-sectional SEM image of the GO/SGQD-2 membrane (inset: elemental mapping of S for the selected area). (e) XRD patterns of the dry GO and GO/SGQD membranes. (f) XRD patterns of the wet GO and GO/SGQD membranes.

(a) Ion exchange capacity of GO/SGQD membranes. (b) Water contact angles of GO, GO/SGQD-1, GO/SGQD-2 and GO/SGQD-3 membranes. (c) and (d) Stability of the GO/SGQD-2 membrane with GO loading amounts of 0.64 g m−2 as a function of immersing time in 1.0 mol L−1 NaOH and 0.1 mol L−1 Na2WO4 simulated alkaline effluents.

(a) Alkali recovery performances of the GO and GO/SGQD membranes at room temperature. The thicknesses of GO, GO/SGQD-1, GO/SGQD-2 and GO/SGQD-3 membranes are 220, 230, 237 and 258 nm, respectively. (b) Alkali recovery performances of the GO/SGQD-2 membrane under different temperatures. (c) Alkali recovery performances of the GO/SGQD-2 membrane for 10 cycles. (d) Alkali recovery performances of the GO/SGQD-2 membrane compared with previously reported literature (see Table S2 for details).

(a) Na contents of GO and GO/SGQD membranes immersed in NaOH/Na2WO4 tested by XPS at an etching depth of 100 nm. (b) Temperature dependence of the OH− permeation rate passing through GO and GO/SGQD-2 membranes. The solid lines are the best fit to calculate the activation energy. (c) Alkali recovery performances of GO, GO/SGQD-2 and GO/PSS membranes. (d) XRD patterns of GO, GO/SGQD-2 and GO/PSS membranes. (e) and (f) Schematic diagrams of the structure of the GO/SGQD and GO/PSS.

head.ce:title: Confined intercalating sulfonated graphene quantum dots into GO laminates for fast alkali recovery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Reaction Engineering, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Anhui, 230009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Reaction Engineering
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Anhui Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Reaction Engineering, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Anhui 230009, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Traditional Chinese Medicine College, Bozhou University, Anhui, 236800, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Traditional Chinese Medicine College, Bozhou University, Anhui 236800, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene oxide (GO) membranes with uniform sub-nanometer interlayer channels have shown great promise in the ion sieving field. However, the lack of ion-transporting sites on the GO nanosheets usually leads to inferior ion permeation and selectivity. Although the intercalation of molecules with ionic groups into GO laminates is easily achieved to incorporate ion exchange sites, this approach inevitably enlarges the interlayer spacing, reducing the ionic sieving capacity. Thus, current methods are limited in breaking the permeation and selectivity “trade-off”. Herein, we report a confined intercalation strategy to inject ample sulfonic acid groups into GO laminates using sulfonated graphene quantum dots (SGQD) as intercalators. SGQD with abundant ion exchange sites assembles on the sp2 domains of GO via π-π interactions and confers the GO membranes with continuous ion conduction pathways. Meanwhile, benefiting from the ultra-small size SGQD as well as its sheet morphology, it can well accommodate in the GO laminates. This strategy fulfills the incorporation of ionic sites without expanding channel width and destructing the two-dimensional (2D) regularity for GO laminates, resulting in simultaneous improvements in ion permeability and selectivity. Correspondingly, the resulting membranes show synergistically improving OH- dialysis coefficients of 3.5 × 10−3-8.2 × 10−3 m h−1 and OH-/WO4 2- separation factors of 56–116 compared with the GO membrane (0.9 × 10−3 m h−1 and 8.4) for separating simulated NaOH/Na2WO4 alkaline effluents. We believe the confined intercalating regulation can act as a versatile and fine way to modify 2D membranes for intensifying ion separation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, many industrial processes involve the sieving of OH- and other anions. For example, during the white tungsten ore (molybdenum ore) smelting technology, a huge amount of alkaline wastewater containing OH− and WO4 2− (MoO4 2−) has been produced. The end wastewater discharge must adhere to strict pH value requirements. Consequently, it becomes imperative to separate OH- from the alkaline wastewater to effectively lower pH values and enable the recycling of alkali [1–4]. Membrane-based separation is quite attractive in recycle alkali due to the prominent advantages of no phase-change, environmental benignity and easy operation [2,5]. Currently, the exploration of membrane materials for treating alkaline effluents mainly focuses on the synthesis or modification of polymeric cation exchange membranes (CEMs) [3,6–13]. However, the permeability and selectivity “trade-off” limitations are ubiquitous in polymeric membranes due to irregular ion transfer channels derived from confusedly tangled macromolecular chains [14–16].

In quite recent years, the two-dimensional (2D) lamellar membranes constructed by stacked graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets have provided a promising opportunity for surpassing the trade-off effect between permeation and selectivity since they have ordered and uniform sub-nanometer channels with sizes comparable to those of target ions, showing huge potential for ion sieving [17–21]. However, the presence of only a very few amounts of cation exchange functional groups (carboxyl groups) at the edges of the GO nanosheets is adverse to the formation of ample and continuous ion transport paths, resulting in limited ion penetration as well as ion sieving [22,23]. To address the above problems, the researchers modified the GO membrane by implanting ion functional groups into 2D laminates. Zhang et al. [24] prepared i-GO membranes by introducing 1-butylsulfonate-3-methylimidazole into the interlaminar channels of the GO membrane. The sulfonic acid groups effectively regulated the chemical environment within the 2D channels and afforded transport sites for the target K+ ions, achieving a K+ flux of 1.36 mol m−2 h−1. However, the selectivity of K+/Mg2+ was only 9.1, primarily attributed to the increasing interlayer spacings of the i-GO membrane compared with the GO membrane. Li et al. [25] integrated sulfonate polyethylenimine (PEI-SO3H) into GO channels and sulfonic acid groups could act as cation conduction sites. The obtained membranes showed improvements in terms of Li+ permeation (0.15 mol m−2 h−1), but the Li+/Mg2+ selectivity only reached around 10. Overall, the current intercalation means have made substantial progresses in enhancing the ion transporting ability of GO membranes, but the selectivity promotion is limited due to the evasive fact that intercalating molecules lead to the expansion of GO interlayers. Accordingly, the current approaches lack abilities to break up the permeation-selectivity “trade-off” constraint of GO membranes for ion sieving.

Citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5%) and sulfamic acid (NH2SO3H, 98%) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Graphite powders (99.8%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 97%), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O, 99.5%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36–38%), titanium trichloride (TiCl3, 15–20%), potassium thiocyanate (KSCN, 98.5%) and potassium persulfate (K2S2O8, 98.5%) were all obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Sodium p-styrene sulfonate (SSS, 98%) was purchased from Energy Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) microporous support membranes with a nominal pore size of 0.45 μm were purchased from Jinteng Experimental Equipment Co., Ltd. (China). Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQD, SGQD, GO and PSS
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQD) were synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid according to the literature (Fig. S3) [26]. Briefly, 2 g of citric acid was put into a 10 mL crucible, heated at 200 °C in the oven until the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to orange. Subsequently, the high-viscosity liquid obtained was dispersed in deionized water and subjected to purification using a 500 Da dialysis bag for 3 days. The purified solution was concentrated by rotary evaporation and then freeze-dried to obtain GQD precursors for the preparation of SGQD. In addition, a portion of the product obtained by pyrolysis was neutralized with 1.0 mol L−1 NaOH, dialyzed, and dried to obtain GQD in the Na+ form for specific characterizations.

SGQD was synthesized via the hydrothermal synthesis method. 2 g GQD and 2.94 g sulfamic acid were dissolved in deionized water (30 mL) and then the mixed solution was transferred to a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor. The reactor was placed in an oven and maintained at 180 °C for 10 h. Afterward, the resulting solution was neutralized with 1.0 mol L−1 NaOH and purified using a 500 Da dialysis bag for 3 days. The excess water was removed by rotary evaporation and then freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain SGQD powders.

GO was prepared by the modified Hummers’ method following the procedure reported in the literature [27]. Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (PSS) was prepared by free radical polymerization of SSS monomers [28]. Details are provided in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GO, GO/SGQD and GO/PSS membranes
ce:section.ce:para: GO was dispersed into deionized water with ultrasonic treatments for 1 h to obtain a uniform dispersion (0.2 mg mL−1).

SGQD was dissolved into deionized water to prepare a homogeneous solution (5 mg mL−1). Then, varied volumes (1.6, 3.2 and 9.6 mL) of the SGQD solution were added to a GO dispersion (4 mL). The pH value of the mixed solution was adjusted to 9–11 using NaOH to prevent agglomeration. The mixture was filtrated through the PVDF membrane supports under vacuum filtration and the obtained lamellar composite membranes were dried in a drying oven at 50 °C for 6 h prior to use. In the preparation process, the theoretical mass ratios of SGQD to GO were 1: 10, 1: 20 and 1: 60, respectively. Correspondingly, the composite membranes were named as GO/SGQD-1, GO/SGQD-2 and GO/SGQD-3, respectively. For comparison, the pure GO membrane and GO/PSS composite membrane were prepared via the same method based on the same GO dispersion volume. The weight ratio of GO and PSS was 1: 20. Notably, 10 mL of GO dispersions were chosen to prepare thicker membranes in order to examine the micro-morphologies clearly. Additionally, when evaluating the alkali recovery performances of GO/SGQD membranes with varying thicknesses, the volumes of GO dispersions were 2, 4, and 10 mL, respectively.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQD and SGQD were obtained by an UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The zeta potentials were conducted on a Nano-ZS90 zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, England). The morphologies and dimensions of GQD and SGQD were characterized via a H800 transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi, Japan) with an operating voltage of 100 kV. High-resolution TEM images were taken out by using a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) and a Talos F200X G2 transmission electron microscope (ThermoFisher, USA), respectively. The element distributions of GO nanosheets and SGQD were mapped by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) on TEM. Atomic force microscope (AFM) images were obtained on a Dimension Icon microscope (Bruker, Germany). Scanning electronic microscopy (SEM) images were observed by a SU8020 scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). The element distributions of membranes were mapped by EDS on SEM. Elemental analysis tests were taken on a Vario EL c elemental analyzer (Elementar Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a LX10-8813 infrared spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained using an ESCALAB 250XI photoelectron spectrometer (ThermoFisher Scientific, USA). The interlayer spacing of the membranes was measured by a X'Pert PRO MPD X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Plamer naco, Netherland). Raman spectra measurements were carried out using LabRAM HR Evolution (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, France) with an incident laser of 532 nm wavelength. Contact angle tests were measured using a GR001PC130D contact angle tester (Jinzhitang, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Alkali recovery performance evaluation using the diffusion dialysis (DD) process
ce:section.ce:para: The DD operation was carried out using a homemade H-shaped device with two compartment cells. The membrane sample with an effective area of 0.5 cm2 was securely placed between a two-compartment cell. One cell was designated as the feed side, and it was filled with 100 mL the mixed solution containing NaOH (1.0 mol L−1) and Na2WO4 (0.1 mol L−1). The other cell was used as the diffusate side, and was filled with 100 mL distilled water. The diffusion process was conducted for 2 h at room temperature and continuously stirred to minimize concentration polarization effects.

The concentration of OH− in the solution was determined by the titration method using HCl solution, and the concentration of WO4 2− in the solution was determined by the thiocyanate spectrophotometric means [29].

The dialysis coefficient (U) is calculated using the given equation:
(1)
U =
M
A t Δ C
Where M is the amount of OH− or WO4 2− transported from feed to diffusate (mol), A is the effective area of the test membrane samples (m2), t is the diffusion time (h), and ΔC is the logarithm average concentration between the two compartments (mol m−3). The value of ΔC is calculated using the following relationship:
(2)
Δ C =
C f 0
−
(
C f t
−
C d t
)
ln
[
C f 0
/
(
C f t
−
C d t
)
]
Where
C f 0
and
C f t
are the feed concentrations at time 0 and t, respectively.
C d t
is the diffusate concentration at time t.

The separation factor (S) is the ratio of dialysis coefficients (U) of two anions (OH− and WO4 2−) present in solutions and calculated by the following formula:
(3)
S =
U
O H
−
U
W O
4
2 −

We conducted an investigation on the OH− permeation of GO and GO/SGQD-2 membranes at different temperatures (25 °C, 40 °C, 55 °C and 70 °C). The GO and GO/SGQD-2 membranes were sealed between a two-compartment cell with an effective test area of 0.5 cm2. The feed side was filled with 100 mL 1.0 mol L−1 NaOH solution, while the permeation side was filled with 100 mL deionized water. The diffusion process was stirred continuously to minimize the influence of concentration polarization and was carried out for 2 h. Finally, the concentration of OH− was determined by titration with 0.1 mol L−1 HCl. The OH− permeation rate P (mol m−2 h−1) was calculated according to the following equation [30]:
(4)
P =
C V
A Δ t
Where C and V are the OH− concentration (mol L−1) and volume (L) of the permeation side, respectively. A is the effective area (m2), and Δt is the permeation time (h). The OH− permeation activation energy E
a (kJ mol−1) was calculated using an Arrhenius equation [31]:
(5)
P = A
e
−
E a
R T
(6)
l n P = l n A −
E a
R T
Where P is the permeation rate of OH− (mol m−2 h−1), A is the pre-exponential factor (mol m−2 h−1), R = 8.314 × 10−3 is the molar gas constant (kJ mol−1 K−1), and T is the feed test temperature (K). According to equation (6), the natural logarithm of the ion permeation rate, lnP, was plotted as a function of (1000/T) and E
a was calculated according to the slope.

SGQD was synthesized via functionalizing GQD with sulfamic acid (Fig. 2 a). To verify the successful preparation of SGQD and check the fine chemical compositions, a range of characterizations such as FTIR, XPS and UV–Vis were performed. As depicted in Fig. 2b, GQD shows typical adsorption bands at 1583 and 1395 cm−1 assigned to the symmetric and antisymmetric stretching vibrations of the peripheral carboxylate groups respectively [32]. The sulfonation of GQD brings about the new peaks at 1140 cm−1 and 624 cm−1, corresponding to the OSO and S–O stretching vibration in SGQD [33,34], indicating that the sulfonic acid groups successfully attach onto GQD. In addition, a typical amide stretching vibration band appears at 1706 cm−1 (N–CO) [35], which verifies that the successful sulfonation of GQD comes from the amide reaction between the amine group of sulfamic acid and the carboxyl group of GQD. The XPS spectra in Fig. 2c shows two additional peaks corresponding to N 1s and S 2p, providing further evidence for the successful introduction of sulfonated molecules in SGQD. Moreover, detailed bonding information of SGQD is obtained by deconvoluting the detected peaks of S, C, and N elements in their respective high-resolution XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. 2d, the two fitting peaks at 168.2 and 169.2 eV emerge in the S 2p high-resolution XPS spectra, assigned to the oxidation state S from sulfonic acid [34]. Besides, the C 1s and N 1s XPS spectra of SGQD present peaks at 288.7 and 401.5 eV respectively [24,36], originating from the N–CO bonding (Fig. 2e and Fig. S4a). This suggests again that the carboxyl group participates in covalent bonding, reacting with the amine group to generate N–CO. As shown in Fig. S4b, both the obtained GQD and SGQD aqueous solution exhibit homogeneous blue fluorescence when exposed to the UV light at 365 nm, consistent with the optical properties reported in other literature [26]. However, the GQD and SGQD solutions display distinct UV–Vis absorption spectra. GQD shows a weak peak at 220 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of CC skeleton and a strong absorption band at 326 nm ascribed to the n-π* transition of CO groups [37,38]. In contrast, the n-π* peak intensity for SGQD is significantly weakened due to the electron-withdrawing effect of sulfonic acid groups [39].

In addition to the chemical composition, the effect of the sulfonation process on the micro-structure of GQD should also be examined. The shape and size of GQD and SGQD are analyzed by TEM, as shown in Fig. 3 a and b. Both GQD and SGQD display uniform nanoflake structures with a size distribution in the scope of 3–8 nm and 9–14 nm, respectively. The larger size of SGQD compared with GQD is due to the sulfonation decoration. Nevertheless, SGQD inherits the good crystallinity of GQD. The high-resolution TEM images in Fig. 3c and d reveal a clear stripe lattice with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphene [39]. As shown in the AFM height profile (Fig. 3e, f and Fig. S5), the thickness of the SGQD is approximately 0.4 nm, corresponding to a mono-layer graphene structure [40]. In addition, the combination of the lateral size (9–14 nm) and thickness information (0.4 nm) of SGQD indicates that it exhibits a high aspect ratio and can be considered as a sheet-like structure [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure and physio-chemical properties of GO/SGQD membranes
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported in the literature that π-π interactions facilitate the 2D membrane assembly [42,43]. There are existing π-π interactions between SGQD and GO nanosheets, as depicted in Fig. 4 a, which can be verified by the fluorescence quenching phenomenon. The SGQD solution shows clear blue fluorescence (Fig. 4b), but an obvious fluorescence quenching phenomenon is observed for the mixing GO + SGQD solution due to the π-π interactions between SGQD and GO (Fig. 4c) [44,45]. Benefitting from the presence of π-π interactions, SGQD tends to distribute on the surface of GO, as shown in Fig. S6, and this interaction force is large enough to stabilize them [43,46]. After being washed for three times, SGQD still exists on the GO nanosheet surfaces, as observed in the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 4d). The attachment of SGQD to GO nanosheets is further confirmed by the elemental mappings of C and S elements. As shown in Fig. 4e and f, the C elements are uniformly distributed on GO nanosheets, and the distribution of the S elements aligns with that of SGQD, which can be attributed to the presence of the C elements in both GO and SGQD, while the S elements are exclusively present in SGQD. Based on the structure of SGQD and π-π interactions, it can be derived that the SGQD inclines to lie parallel onto the GO nanosheet surfaces, availing for the insertion of SGQD into the GO interlayers. For achieving the smooth preparation of GO/SGQD membranes, the pH values of the mixing SGQD and GO suspensions should be thoroughly adjusted due to the different pH dependent zeta potentials situations of SGQD and GO dispersions. As shown in Fig. 4g, the zeta potentials of GO are negative in the pH range of 1–13, attributed to the presence of epoxy groups and carboxyl groups that anchor onto the surface. Comparatively, SGQD exhibits positive zeta potentials when the pH value falls in the range of 1–5, ascribing to the protonation of the N atom in the amide units under acidic conditions [39]. Hence, in acidic conditions, GO and SGQD are prone to flocculation due to electrostatic interactions (Fig. S7), which is adverse to the formation of regular and ordered layered structures. In order to obtain homogeneous solutions for the membrane assembly, it is necessary to adjust the pH of the mixed dispersion in the scope of 9–11. The uniform solution of SGQD and GO was deposited on the PVDF substrate by the pressure-assisted self-assembly method to prepare the GO/SGQD lamellar membrane (Fig. S8). Further details regarding the FTIR, XPS, Raman spectra and zeta potential analyses of the GO/SGQD membranes can be found in the supporting information (Fig. S9).

As shown in Fig. 5 a and b and Fig. S10, the surface morphology of the GO/SGQD membrane is similar to that of the original GO membrane. Both GO and GO/SGQD membranes display clearly visible typical wrinkles caused by stacked nanosheets boundaries and continuous coverage on the substrate without any detectable pinholes and cracks. The thickness information was recorded by the cross-sectional image presented in Fig. S11. When the loading amounts of GO is 0.64 g m−2, the thicknesses of GO, GO/SGQD-1, GO/SGQD-2 and GO/SGQD-3 membranes are 220, 230, 237 and 258 nm, respectively. It can be found that the thickness of the GO/SGQD-2 membrane only increases by 7% compared to that of the GO membrane, tentatively suggesting the incorporation of SGQD has no effects on the interlayer spacings of GO membranes. The linear relationships for the loading amount of GO and GO/SGQD membranes versus thickness are shown in Fig. S12. To observe the cross-sectional structure of the GO/SGQD-2 membrane more clearly, thicker membranes based on the same proportion of SGQD were prepared and observed by SEM (with a volume of 10 mL for the GO dispersion). As shown in Fig. 5c and d, the laminar structure of the GO/SGQD-2 membrane is well-preserved, similar to the original GO membrane. This retention of the laminar structure can be attributed to the π-π interactions between SGQD and GO. The elemental mapping EDS reveals that the characteristic S elements from SGQD are uniformly distributed over the whole cross section, indicating that SGQD has been successfully incorporated within the channels of the GO membrane (as illustrated in the inset Fig. 5d).

The effects of SGQD intercalation on the d-spacings of GO laminates was further examined by XRD patterns. As shown in Fig. 5e, the pristine GO membrane has a strong diffraction peak at 10.9°, corresponding to a d-spacing of 0.81 nm. Correspondingly, GO/SGQD-1, GO/SGQD-2 and GO/SGQD-3 membranes exhibit d-spacings of 0.80, 0.81 and 0.85 nm, respectively. Compared with the GO membrane, the largest d-spacing of GO/SGQD-3 membrane merely increases by 0.05 nm. To simulate the realistic operating conditions of the membranes, the wetted membranes were further investigated. The interlayer spacing variations of the wet membranes follow the same trend as those of dry membranes (Fig. 5f). The d-spacings of the wet GO/SGQD-1 and GO/SGQD-2 membranes are comparable to that of the wet GO membrane. The obviously expanding d-spacing of the wetted GO/SGQD-3 membrane can be attributed to the fact that excessive SGQD incorporation causes strong water sorption [37,47]. A detailed discussion on the reasons for the widening of the layer spacing can be obtained in the supporting information (Fig. S13). These results make us believe that the confined SGQD intercalators are extraordinarily effective in achieving controlling interlayer spacings for GO lamellar membranes.

The ion exchange capacity (IEC) is an essential parameter used to describe the level of ion exchange group grafting in the membrane, which is of significant importance for facilitating fast ion migrations. The IEC values of GO/SGQD membranes were calculated by the amount of S elements in SGQD. Although the IEC values of SGQD are as high as 5.49 mmol g−1, the IEC values of GO/SGQD-1, GO/SGQD-2 and GO/SGQD-3 membranes are 0.78, 1.02 and 1.62 mmol g−1, respectively (Fig. 6 a and Table S1). Apart from IEC, the membrane material’ hydrophilicity has an important effect on ion transport as well. As shown in Fig. 6b, the decreasing water contact angles of GO/SGQD membranes compared with the GO membrane manifest that the introduction of SGQD enhances hydrophilicity. The increasing hydrophilicity will be expected to facilitate OH− transfer via constructing more hydrogen-bonding networks [7].

The static stability of GO and GO/SGQD membranes in simulated alkaline wastewater was investigated before evaluating the alkali recovery performance of membranes in DD mode (Fig. 6c, d and Fig. S14). These membranes were immersed in 1.0 mol L−1 NaOH and 0.1 mol L−1 Na2WO4 mixed solution for 80 days and no decomposition was observed, hinting that the membranes show good stability in alkaline environments and could be applied to recycle alkali.

The pristine GO membranes with various thicknesses (0.1, 0.2, 0.5 and 1 μm) were first investigated for NaOH/Na2WO4 separation. As shown in Fig. S15a, the GO/SGQD membrane presents gradually descending UOH − values ranging from 1.3 × 10−3 to 0.4 × 10−3 m h−1 with increasing thicknesses. Correspondingly, the S values increase from 4.6 to 20.2. Taking permeability and selectivity into comprehensive consideration, the GO membrane with any thicknesses always generates unsatisfactory alkali recovery performances.

In this work, we propose a confined intercalation strategy by embedding SGQD with dense ion exchange groups into 2D GO channels to improve the alkali recovery ability of the GO membranes. Fig. 7 a shows the variation of OH− permeation and separation performance of GO/SGQD membranes with different SGQD doping ratios. Compared with the GO membrane, both UOH − and S values for GO/SGQD membranes demonstrate dramatic enhancements. The UOH − values lie in the range of 3.5 × 10−3 to 8.2 × 10−3 m h−1, which is about 4–9 times higher than the original GO membrane (0.9 × 10−3 m h−1), and more importantly, the S values of GO/SGQD membranes (56–116) are 7–13 times higher than those of the GO membrane (8.4), confirming the advantage of GO/SGQD membranes for alkali recovery. Specifically, as increasing intercalation SGQD amounts from the GO/SGQD-1 to GO/SGQD-3 membrane, the UOH − and S values deliver gradually increasing and decreasing trends respectively. The increasingly improving UOH − originates from more anionic groups (SO3 −) existing in GO/SGQD-3, as confirmed by the IEC levels. Comparatively, the gradually dropping S stems from the slightly enlarged 2D channels in GO/SGQD-3, as observed in the above XRD patterns. The GO/SGOD-2 membrane gives rise to balanced alkali recovery performances.

For GO/SGQD membranes, the effects of membrane thicknesses and operation temperature on UOH − and S have been further investigated. Based on the optimal doping ratio of SGQD, when the membrane thickness increases from 0.12 μm to 0.60 μm (Fig. S15b), the UOH − value decreases from 6.86 × 10−3 to 2.04 × 10−3 m h−1, while the S value increases from 50 to 120 due to the increasing 2D mass transfer channel numbers. As the working temperature rising from 25 to 70 °C, UOH − increases and S declines for the GO/SGQD-2 membrane gradually. The temperature dependence of alkali recovery properties for the GO/SGQD membrane are in agreement with those of alkali recovery membranes [7].

For practical alkali recovery applications, the membranes should hold sufficient stability. The comparisons of XRD patterns for the GO/SGQD-2 membrane before and after alkali recovery measurements suggest that the ordered lamellar structures are not broken by the DD process (Fig. S16a). And the micro-structures are almost unchanged as well confirmed by the cross-sectional SEM image (Fig. S16b). These findings indicate that the membrane possesses structural stability and can sustain their integrity during alkali recovery processes. Long-term stability under operating conditions is an important indicator to evaluate the realistic application prospect of separation membranes. The results show that the GO/SGQD-2 membrane exhibits fairly stable performances over 10 repetitive cycles, and the UOH − and S change only slightly (Fig. 7c). Compared with the reported literature for alkali recovery membranes, the OH− separation performances of the GO/SGQD-2 membrane in this work is superior to those of most of the polymeric CEMs, although the IEC of most polymeric CEMs are higher than that of the GO/SGQD-2 membrane (Fig. 7d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigations of fast separation mechanisms for GO/SGQD membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent properties of GO/SGQD membranes for alkali recovery are supposed to result from the optimized physicochemical micro-environments of nanochannels. In contrast with GO, GO/SGQD has high contents of sulfonic acid groups surrounding the SGQD, which is in favor of the formation of continuous Na+ transport paths as depicted in Fig. S17. To keep the permeation side electroneutrality, mono-valent OH− with a smaller size preferentially penetrates across GO/SGQD membranes along with Na+ permeation. This point is validated by the detective Na contents within 2D channels [7,48]. As analyzed by XPS, the Na percentage in GO is 1.22%, while that in the GO/SGQD-2 membrane is 2.44%, which mediately proves that the Na+ penetration into 2D channels of the former is much more difficult than those of the latter (Fig. 8 a). On the other hand, the enhanced hydrophilicity of GO/SGQD compared to GO also facilitates quicker OH− permeation. We examined the OH− permeation of the GO/SGQD-2 and GO membrane at different temperatures (Fig. 8b) and Ea of OH− transports was calculated according to the Arrhenius equation. As revealed in the previous reports, Ea reflects the ion transport capacity through the nanochannels [19,31]. The Ea values for OH− transport through the GO and GO/SGQD-2 membranes are 38.05 kJ mol−1 and 26.11 kJ mol−1, indicating that OH− passes more easily through the GO/SGQD-2 membrane than through the GO membrane. This further confirms that the GO/SGQD-2 membrane is favorable for enhancing UOH −.

Apart from the promising OH− diffusion, it is exciting that the S values of GO/SGQD membranes are about 10 times higher than that of the GO membrane. In order to probe the role of the SGQD confined regulation strategy for obviously improving OH−/WO4 2− sieving, we carried out a control experiment to select sodium polystyrene sulfonate (PSS) as an intercalator instead of SGQD to incorporate rich sulfonic acid groups into GO laminates. The GO/PSS membrane was prepared with comparable IEC with the GO/SGQD-2 membrane. As shown in Fig. 8c, the UOH − value of the GO/PSS membrane is 6.8 × 10−3 m h−1, which is slightly higher than that of the GO/SGQD-2 membrane (6.0 × 10−3 m h−1). However, the S value of the GO/PSS membrane declines about 10 times in sharp contrast to that of the GO/SGQD-2 membrane. The introduction of PSS in the GO membrane enhances UOH − at the expense of selectivity. The GO/SGQD and GO/PSS membranes possess the same micro-chemical environments within 2D channels and thus, we speculate that the physical structure disparity contributes to unlike sieving abilities. As presented in Fig. 8d and Fig. S18, the GO/PSS laminate has a larger interlayer spacing than the GO/SGQD counterpart under both dry and solvated states ascribed to the inconsistent molecule configuration between PSS and SGQD (Fig. 8e and f). Furthermore, it is evident that the average full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the wet GO/PSS membrane (0.945°) is larger compared to that of the GO/SGQD-2 membrane (0.848°) (Table S3). This suggests that the interlayer channels of the wet GO/PSS membrane are wider and less ordered, leading to lower selectivity for alkaline recovery [49]. Comparatively, the SGQD intercalator has a considerable area of conjugated structures with a longitudinal dimension of 0.4 nm, which is embedded within the GO laminates without expanding the interlayer spacing. Thus, the promising selectivity of the GO/SGQD membrane synergistically originates from discrepant electrostatic repulsion forces of sulfonic acid groups to mono-valent OH− and di-valent WO4 2− ions, as well as the retention of the intrinsic interlayer spacing of GO laminates.

In summary, we have successfully demonstrated a confined intercalation strategy to embed SGQD into GO laminates, resulting in highly efficient alkali recovery performances. Benefiting from the densely distributed sulfonic acid groups around SGQD as well as the sheet morphology, the elaborately constructed GO/SGQD membranes not only have well-structured interlayer channels and inherent GO spacings, but also provide ample continuous transport paths for Na+. Therefore, the GO/SGQD membrane can transport more OH− according to the electroneutrality principle and better differentiate OH−/WO4 2− by the electrostatic repulsion process. For treating simulated industrial wastewater NaOH/Na2WO4, the GO/SGQD membranes show ideal performances with UOH − values of 3.5 × 10−3-8.2 × 10−3 m h−1 and S values of 56–116, far outperforming the GO membrane (0.9 × 10−3 m h−1 and 8.4). The resulting GO/SGQD membranes achieve a considerable improvement in selectivity while enhancing ion permeability in alkali recovery, successfully breaking the “trade-off” limitation regarding permeability and selectivity.
Jingjing Gu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing original draft.
Ziqiang Hong: Investigation, Data curation.
Suixin Zhang: Investigation, Data curation.
Rui Jia: Data curation, Validation.
Ruonan Tan: Validation, Methodology.
Yong Li: Investigation, Methodology.
Hongwei Shao: Investigation, Methodology.
Shichao Zhang: Validation.
Xinxin Li: Validation.
Peng Cui: Conceptualization, Supervision.
Jin Ran: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that we have no conflicts of interest to this work. We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project has been partially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2022YFB3805102), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22278105, 21978062).
